title: Intermediate SQL Quiz — Query Practice
description: A 15-question intermediate SQL test with query-based items to practice real-world SQL reasoning.

questions.question:
Given table orders(order_id, customer_id, amount) and customers(customer_id, name), which query returns customers who have placed at least one order?

SELECT c.customer_id, c.name FROM customers c ...

options.text: LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.order_id IS NOT NULL
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
RIGHT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
CROSS JOIN orders o

questions.explanation: An INNER JOIN returns only customers that have matching orders; LEFT JOIN with IS NOT NULL is more verbose but INNER JOIN is the standard answer.

questions.question:
Which of the following queries returns the top 3 highest-amount orders per customer in PostgreSQL?

Assume orders(order_id, customer_id, amount).

options.text: SELECT * FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC) rn FROM orders) t WHERE rn <= 3
SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY amount DESC LIMIT 3
SELECT customer_id, MAX(amount) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id LIMIT 3
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id) * FROM orders ORDER BY customer_id, amount

questions.explanation: Using ROW_NUMBER() partitioned by customer and filtering rn <= 3 returns top 3 per customer. DISTINCT ON is useful but option D as written doesn't pick top 3.

questions.question:
What does this query return?

SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY department HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;

options.text: Departments with more than 5 employees whose salary is > 50000
Departments with average salary > 50000
All departments with employees and their counts
Employees with salary > 50000

questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows before aggregation; HAVING then filters groups where the count of filtered rows is > 5.

questions.question: Consider table sales(id, sale_date, amount). Which index will best speed up queries that filter by sale_date range and order by amount?

options.text: CREATE INDEX idx1 ON sales(sale_date)
CREATE INDEX idx2 ON sales(amount)
CREATE INDEX idx3 ON sales(sale_date, amount)
CREATE INDEX idx4 ON sales(amount, sale_date)

questions.explanation: An index starting with sale_date then amount supports range filter on sale_date and ordering by amount for that range.

questions.question: Given products(id, category, price), which query finds categories with the highest average price?

options.text: SELECT category FROM products GROUP BY category ORDER BY AVG(price) DESC LIMIT 1
SELECT category, AVG(price) FROM products WHERE price = (SELECT MAX(price) FROM products)
SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products ORDER BY AVG(price) DESC
SELECT category FROM products GROUP BY category HAVING AVG(price) = MAX(price)

questions.explanation: Ordering groups by AVG(price) and taking LIMIT 1 returns the category with highest average price.

questions.question:
Which query correctly uses a window function to compute a running total of amount ordered by created_at?

Table: orders(id, created_at, amount)

options.text: SELECT id, created_at, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS running_total FROM orders
SELECT id, created_at, amount, SUM(amount) FROM orders ORDER BY created_at
SELECT id, created_at, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY created_at) FROM orders
SELECT id, created_at, amount, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY created_at) FROM orders

questions.explanation: SUM() as a window function with ORDER BY created_at computes a running total across rows in chronological order.

questions.question: Which statement about transactions is correct?

options.text: READ UNCOMMITTED prevents phantom reads
SERIALIZABLE is the strictest isolation level and avoids anomalies
COMMIT rolls back the transaction
SAVEPOINT discards the transaction

questions.explanation: SERIALIZABLE is the strongest standard isolation level, preventing many concurrency anomalies. COMMIT makes changes permanent.

questions.question: Which query is the most efficient way to check existence of at least one row matching a condition?

options.text: SELECT COUNT(*) > 0 FROM table WHERE condition
SELECT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM table WHERE condition)
SELECT * FROM table WHERE condition LIMIT 1
SELECT TOP 1 1 FROM table WHERE condition

questions.explanation: EXISTS is optimized to stop at first match and is more efficient than counting all rows. (Note: TOP syntax is dialect-specific.)

questions.question: Given orders(id, order_date, amount), which query removes duplicate orders keeping the one with highest amount per (id) in PostgreSQL?

options.text: DELETE FROM orders o USING (SELECT id, MAX(amount) AS max_amount FROM orders GROUP BY id) m WHERE o.id = m.id AND o.amount < m.max_amount
DELETE FROM orders WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT MAX(id) FROM orders GROUP BY id)
TRUNCATE TABLE orders
DROP TABLE orders

questions.explanation: Using DELETE ... USING with a grouped subquery allows deleting rows with amount less than the max per id.

questions.question:
Which query correctly pivots month sales into columns for months Jan and Feb (simplified)?

Table: sales(month, amount)

options.text: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Jan, SUM(CASE WHEN month='Feb' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Feb FROM sales
SELECT month, SUM(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY month PIVOT
SELECT * FROM sales PIVOT (SUM(amount) FOR month IN ('Jan','Feb'))
SELECT month, amount FROM sales WHERE month IN ('Jan','Feb')

questions.explanation: Using conditional aggregation with CASE is a portable way to pivot months into columns. PIVOT syntax is dialect-specific.

questions.question: Which index strategy reduces index size while keeping fast lookups for low-cardinality boolean column active?

options.text: Create a btree index on active
Create a partial index where active = true
Create a unique index on active
Do not index at all

questions.explanation: Partial indexes (e.g., WHERE active = true) reduce index size and are efficient when most rows are false.

questions.question:
What is the result of this query?

WITH RECURSIVE t AS (SELECT 1 AS n UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM t WHERE n < 3) SELECT * FROM t;

options.text: 1,2,3 rows
An infinite loop
An error because recursive CTEs are not allowed
Only 1 row

questions.explanation: Recursive CTE generates rows 1 through 3 because the recursion stops at n < 3 when n becomes 3.

questions.question:
Which query avoids a full table scan when searching a JSONB column for a top-level key 'status' = 'active' in PostgreSQL?

Table: items(data JSONB)

options.text: SELECT * FROM items WHERE data->>'status' = 'active'
CREATE INDEX idx ON items ((data->>'status')) ; SELECT * FROM items WHERE data->>'status' = 'active'
SELECT * FROM items WHERE data @> '{"status":"active"}'
Both B and C with appropriate index

questions.explanation: Creating a functional index on (data->>'status') allows equality lookups without full scan. JSONB containment @> can also be indexed but B is the direct solution.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about ANALYZE / VACUUM (Postgres)?

options.text: ANALYZE updates table statistics used by planner; VACUUM reclaims space from deleted rows
ANALYZE deletes rows; VACUUM updates statistics
Both commands rebuild indexes
Neither affects query planning

questions.explanation: ANALYZE gathers statistics for the planner; VACUUM reclaims storage from dead tuples. Both are important for performance.

questions.question: You need to safely add a NOT NULL column with default to a large table without long locks. Which approach is best?

options.text: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN new_col TYPE DEFAULT x NOT NULL
Add column nullable, backfill in batches, then ALTER TABLE SET NOT NULL
Create new table with column and copy data
Drop the table and recreate with new column

questions.explanation: Adding the column as nullable and backfilling in controlled batches then marking NOT NULL avoids long exclusive locks on large tables.
